docs.title: Home
docs.text:
The place where I privately collect my inchoate thoughts, ideas, scripts, and other miscellany.

Fully formed thoughts can be found at: Girl in Progress or Abby Jane's Knowledge Base

But, honestly, if you didn't know that already, then you shouldn't be here in the first place.

docs.title: Create function
docs.text:
set -U GIT_HOME_<name of repository> "/Users/abby/Library/Mobile\ Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/Programming/.git/.<name of repository>

funced --save <name of repository>

function <name of repository> --description 'Git for <reository>'
    /usr/bin/git --git-dir=$GIT_HOME_<NAME OF REPOSITORY>/.git --work-tree=<directory with files for repository> $argv
end

docs.title: Create local repository
docs.text:
**Note:**For ease: name ofrepository = repotest

repotest init $GIT_HOME_REPOTEST'

docs.title: Create remote repository
docs.text:
gh repo create

? What would you like to do?  [Use arrows to move, type to filter]
> Create a new repository on GitHub from scratch
  Create a new repository on GitHub from a template repository
  Push an existing local repository to GitHub

? Repository name repotest

? Description Place for tests

? Visibility  [Use arrows to move, type to filter]
  Public
> Private
  Internal

? Would you like to add a README file? (y/N) N

? Would you like to add a README file? No

? Would you like to add a .gitignore? No

? Would you like to add a license? No

? This will create "repotest" as a private repository on GitHub. Continue? (Y/n) Y
✓ Created repository abby/testrepo on GitHub
  https://github.com/abby/testrepo

? Clone the new repository locally? No

docs.title: Configure files to track
docs.text:
testrepo config --local status.showUntrackedFiles no

cd <directory with files for repository>

repotest add <directories or files>

Examples:

testrepo add test1/ --Adds entire directory and subdirectories

testrepo add test1/*.txt --Adds only the .txt files in that directory

testrepo add $(find test1/test2/ -type f -maxdepth 1) --Adds only the files in test1/test2 directory without adding any files from test1/test2/test3 subdirectory

docs.title: Commit files
docs.text: testrepo commit -m "<Description of commit>"

docs.title: Configure push and push files
docs.text:
testrepo remote add origin <url to repo created earlier>

testrepo branch -M main

testrepo push -u origin main

docs.title: Check status of local repository
docs.text: testrepo status

docs.title: Section 1 — National Citizenship and Supremacy
docs.text: The Fourteenth Amendment is reaffirmed as a guarantee of national citizenship and as a binding limitation on the States. No State shall invoke sovereignty, reserved powers, dignity, or immunity as a basis to evade or diminish the rights secured by that Amendment.

docs.title: Section 2 — Privileges or Immunities
docs.text: The privileges or immunities of citizens of the United States include all rights secured by this Constitution and by laws enacted pursuant thereto. No State shall abridge such privileges or immunities. The privileges or immunities secured by this Constitution, and by laws enacted pursuant thereto, shall apply to all persons and all territory subject to the jurisdiction or authority of the United States. No interpretation of this Constitution shall construe this clause narrowly or as without independent force.

docs.title: Section 3 — Due Process
docs.text: No State shall deprive any person of life, liberty, or property without due process of law. This guarantee secures substantive as well as procedural protections and applies to all rights secured by this Constitution.

docs.title: Section 4 — Equal Protection
docs.text: The Equal Protection of the laws guarantees equal civil status and equal protection against arbitrary, discriminatory, or unequal state action. No classification by a State shall be upheld unless consistent with this guarantee.

docs.title: Section 5 — Incorporation
docs.text: All rights enumerated in this Constitution are applicable to the States. No distinction shall be drawn between incorporated and unincorporated rights.

docs.title: Section 6 — Enforcement Power (Restored)
docs.text: Congress shall have power to enforce the provisions of this Amendment by appropriate legislation. Such power shall include the authority to define, prevent, remedy, and deter violations of this Amendment. This authority shall not be limited by judicial doctrines requiring congruence, proportionality, narrow tailoring, or deference to state sovereignty.

docs.title: Section 7 — Disqualification Clause Enforcement
docs.text: The disqualification prescribed by Section 3 of the Fourteenth Amendment is self-executing. No prior criminal conviction, impeachment, or congressional action shall be required for its enforcement. Congress may by law provide procedures for the determination and enforcement of such disqualification, but the absence of such procedures shall not bar its application by any court or election authority with jurisdiction, notwithstanding the absence of implementing legislation.

docs.title: Section 8 — Supersession
docs.text: Any prior judicial interpretation inconsistent with this Amendment or with the original purpose and enforcement authority of the Fourteenth Amendment is hereby superseded.

docs.title: Section 9 — Citizenship
docs.text:
All persons born in the United States and subject to its laws are citizens of the United States and of the State wherein they reside.

A person born in the United States shall be deemed subject to the jurisdiction of the United States at birth regardless of the citizenship, nationality, immigration status, or lawful presence of the person’s parents.

The phrase “subject to the jurisdiction thereof” excludes only persons born:

  1. to foreign diplomatic officers accredited to the United States;

  2. to persons exercising sovereign authority on behalf of a foreign power; or

  3. to members of an invading or occupying military force.

No other exception to birthright citizenship shall be recognized as a constitutional requirement.

Congress may by law provide for the acquisition of citizenship on terms more permissive than those set forth in this Section.

docs.title: Section 1
docs.text: No amendment proposed pursuant to Article V shall be invalidated by the passage of time.

docs.title: Section 2
docs.text: An amendment becomes part of this Constitution upon ratification by three-fourths of the States, or by conventions in three-fourths thereof, existing at the time of ratification.

docs.title: Section 1
docs.text: The judicial power of the United States shall extend to all cases and controversies within the jurisdiction of the federal courts as established by this Constitution and by law. The requirement of a case or controversy shall be satisfied where adverse parties present a concrete dispute capable of judicial resolution.

docs.title: Section 2
docs.text: No court of the United States shall decline to exercise jurisdiction otherwise properly conferred on the ground that a question presented is political, prudential, or inappropriate for judicial determination.

docs.title: Section 3
docs.text: Congress may by law define and regulate the jurisdiction of the federal courts, including standards of standing, ripeness, and mootness. No judicially created doctrine shall limit such jurisdiction except as expressly provided in this Constitution.

docs.title: Section 4
docs.text: Where jurisdiction is proper and facts sufficient to resolve a dispute are established, the courts of the United States shall decide the questions of law presented.

docs.title: Section 5
docs.text: Any prior judicial interpretation inconsistent with this amendment is hereby superseded.

docs.title: Section 1 — Covered Officers
docs.text:
The pardon power of the President shall not extend to any person who, at the time of the conduct for which pardon is sought:

(a) held an office of the United States whose appointment required confirmation by the Senate;

(b) served in an acting, interim, or temporary capacity in any such office; or

(c) served in the Executive Office of the President or in any successor office designated by Congress, regardless of title or organizational form.

docs.title: Section 2 — Specificity Requirement
docs.text:
Every pardon shall:

(a) identify the individual by legal name;

(b) specify the conduct pardoned; and

(c) identify the federal statute or statues, including section numbers, to which the pardon applies.

No pardon may use general, omnibus, or open-ended language such as “all offenses against the United States.”

docs.title: Section 3 — No Self-Amnesty and No Class Pardons
docs.text: No President may pardon himself or herself. No pardon may be issued to any class of persons defined by office, agency, function, or status.

docs.title: Section 4 — Effect of Violation
docs.text: Any pardon issued in violation of this Amendment shall have no legal force.

docs.title: Section 5 — Preservation of Clemency
docs.text: Nothing in this Amendment shall restrict the President’s power to grant pardons to persons not described in Section 1, except as limited by the requirements of Section 2.

docs.title: Section 6 — Effective Date
docs.text: This Amendment shall take effect upon ratification.

docs.title: Section 1
docs.text: The Eleventh Amendment to the Constitution of the United States is hereby repealed.

docs.title: Section 2
docs.text: Neither the United States nor any State shall be immune, by reason of sovereignty, from suit in any court of the United States or of any court of any State arising under this Constitution, the laws of the United States, or treaties made under their authority.

docs.title: Section 3
docs.text: Congress shall have power to regulate the remedies available in such suits, including limitations on damages, prospective relief, and procedural requirements.

docs.title: Section 4
docs.text: No doctrine of sovereign immunity, whether derived from common law, constitutional interpretation, or judicial construction, shall bar jurisdiction otherwise granted by this Constitution or by law.

docs.title: Section 5
docs.text: No immunity from suit or liability shall be recognized as a constitutional requirement unless expressly stated in this Constitution.

docs.title: Section 1
docs.text: The rights, privileges, and immunities secured by this Constitution, as amended, shall not be denied or abridged by the United States or by any State on account of gender.

docs.title: Section 2
docs.text: Congress shall have power to enforce this article by appropriate legislation. All existing and proposed laws, treaties, and judicial decisions under this Constitution shall be consistent with this article.

docs.title: Section 3
docs.text: For purposes of this article, gender includes sex, gender identity, gender expression, and any classification or treatment based thereon.

docs.title: Section 1
docs.text: The executive power of the United States is vested in the President and such officers, agencies, and instrumentalities as may be established by law. The vesting of executive power in the President shall not be construed to require that all persons exercising executive functions be subject to the President’s direction, supervision, or removal at will.

docs.title: Section 2
docs.text: Congress may by law establish executive officers and agencies whose heads or members may be appointed for fixed terms and removed only for cause, or in such manner as Congress shall provide. The existence or exercise of executive authority by such officers or agencies shall not be deemed inconsistent with this Constitution.

docs.title: Section 3
docs.text: No interpretation of this Constitution shall recognize an inherent or exclusive presidential authority to control, direct, or remove all executive officers solely by virtue of the Vesting Clause of Article II.

docs.title: Section 4
docs.text: Any prior judicial interpretation inconsistent with this amendment is hereby superseded.

docs.title: Thoughts on the impending Rule 12(b) Motion to Dismiss
12(b)(6)
docs.text:
Defense: "Direct public employment by a state actor is not a ‘common calling’ protected by Article IV"

Defendants’ motion analyzes this case as though Plaintiff had asserted a Title VII–style claim to public employment or a constitutional right to be hired by a state actor. The Complaint makes no such claim. Plaintiff does not allege a right to employment, nor does he challenge Defendants’ authority to require on-campus work for positions so designated.

The position at issue was expressly advertised as “remote-eligible.” Defendants were willing to consider and hire candidates who could work on campus, regardless of state residence. Plaintiff was denied only the opportunity to be considered for remote work based solely on his residence in Alabama, pursuant to a categorical policy excluding residents of certain states from remote eligibility.

Plaintiff alleges that this residency-based exclusion from consideration for remote work burdens the right to pursue a common calling on equal terms, in violation of the Privileges and Immunities Clause of Article IV.

docs.title: Piper
docs.text:
Piper specifically rejected as a 'substantial burden':

  • administrative convenience,
  • speculative enforcement difficulties,
  • generalized concerns about oversight,
  • blanked exclusions justified by "we've always done it this way."

Categorical exclusions are suspect, and the state (or state actor) must justify why residents of certain states, as a class, pose a distinct problem.

Defendants assert that their policy “meets the requisite standard,” but that conclusion assumes the answer to the very question presented. Under Camden and Piper, once a plaintiff plausibly alleges a residency-based burden on a common calling, the burden shifts to the state to demonstrate a substantial reason for the discrimination and a close fit between means and ends. Defendants’ motion identifies no such justification on the face of the pleadings.

docs.title: Camden
docs.text: Opinion

docs.title: 12(b)(2): Personal Jurisdiction
docs.text:
Defendants did not merely make information available on the internet; they invited and processed applications from Alabama residents through a structured, interactive system, and then applied a residency-based policy to those applicants.

Defendants characterize their contacts with Alabama as merely passive internet activity. But the position at issue was advertised through an interactive online system that invited applicants to create accounts, submit applications, and engage in individualized review. Defendants knowingly accepted applications from Alabama residents and then applied a residency-based exclusion to those applicants. Such conduct is not “passive” under the Zippo framework and reflects purposeful engagement with the forum.

Defendants did not merely make information available on the internet; they invited and processed applications from Alabama residents through a structured, interactive system, and then applied a residency-based policy to those applicants.

Plaintiff does not rely on his residence or the location of injury alone, but on Defendants’ deliberate engagement with Alabama residents through an interactive application process and application of a residency-based exclusion giving rise to this claim.

Defendants knowingly accepted applications from Alabama residents and applied a residency-based exclusion to those residents through their hiring system.

Defendants argue that their conduct was not directed toward Alabama. But the policy at issue operates by differentiating among states, approving residents of some states for remote eligibility while categorically excluding others. In that sense, the policy is necessarily directed at states qua states, including Alabama.

docs.title: Github Accounts
docs.text: A non-exhaustive list of github users to follow

docs.title: 🧱 Static sites, Jekyll, “boring but elegant” web
docs.text:
GitHub Pages–adjacent, clean, sane people

  • @github/pages-gem

    • The canonical reference for what GitHub Pages actually supports. If something breaks locally vs Pages, this repo usually explains why.

  • @jekyll

    • Not flashy, but good signal on where Jekyll is going (and not going).

  • @mmistakes (Michael Rose)

    • Author of Minimal Mistakes theme, but more importantly: excellent examples of disciplined Jekyll structure and documentation.

  • @simonw (Simon Willison)

    • Static sites, personal publishing, data → HTML pipelines, zero BS. Very much your “small tools, sharp edges” vibe.

docs.title: 📝 Documentation, writing, developer ergonomics
docs.text:
People who treat docs as first-class

  • @danielecook

    • Thoughtful writing about docs, tooling, and long-lived systems.

  • @docwhat

    • Docs, accessibility, clarity, and technical writing culture.

  • @wilfred

    • Known for diff tooling and thoughtful posts on developer experience.

  • @karlhorky

    • Rails-y background, but very strong opinions about maintainable docs and tooling.

docs.title: 🐍 Python (practical, not hype)
docs.text:
• @kennethreitz

    • Requests, Pipenv, tooling that favors humans.

  • @tiangolo

    • FastAPI, excellent README hygiene, great examples of API documentation done right.

  • @nvbn

    • Small sharp Python tools, often very readable.

docs.title: 🦀 Rust (approachable, not macho)
docs.text:
Since you’ve been flirting with Rust but not in a “rewrite the world” way:

  • @burntsushi (Andrew Gallant)

    • Rust done responsibly. Deep explanations, zero hype, lots of text processing.

  • @dtolnay

    • Procedural macros, Serde internals — heavy stuff, but impeccably documented.

  • @matklad

    • Rust analyzer, thoughtful design writing, very “engineer’s engineer.”

docs.title: 🧰 Unix, DevOps, infra minimalism
docs.text:
People who hate unnecessary complexity:

  • @jpetazzo

    • Containers, infra history, thoughtful critiques of trends.

  • @iximiuz

    • Excellent explainer repos on Linux, containers, networking.

  • @leandromoreira

    • Computer architecture explainers that are actually readable.

docs.title: Media
docs.text:
For Movies: yts.do

For TV and Music The Pirate Bay

docs.title: The Midwest
docs.text:
1. Default Culture, Not Negotiated Culture

Midwestern culture operates as a default system. Shared norms are assumed rather than negotiated. People are socialized to believe most others share the same expectations, so friction is managed preemptively instead of explicitly.

By contrast, Southern, Appalachian, or racially mixed regions require constant cultural negotiation. There, ambiguity is owned by the speaker. In the Midwest, ambiguity is collaboratively smoothed over.

This explains: • verbalized decision trees (“Light or Heavy?”), • redundancy framed as kindness, • and the absence of overt boundary-testing.

⸻

  1. Politeness as Friction Management, Not Relational Repair

Midwestern “nice” is procedural, not relational. • Interactions end once discomfort is neutralized, not once mutual recognition is confirmed. • Minimal acknowledgments (“ope”) complete the repair without waiting for response or absolution. • There is no pause for dignity exchange; the goal is to move on cleanly.

This contrasts with Southern politeness, which is high-context and relational, where pauses, acknowledgment, and mutual recognition matter.

⸻

  1. Prosody and Affect Are Intentionally Flattened

Midwestern speech de-emphasizes stress, elongates non-salient vowels, and resolves downward. Prosody is used to lower emotional temperature, not to signal meaning or hierarchy.

For someone from a river-based or high-context culture, this feels rhythmically wrong—like clapping off-beat—because emphasis does not align with semantic importance.

⸻

  1. Curiosity Is Deferred, Not Absent

People avoid asking personal questions until invited. Non-intrusion is a form of respect.

Rapport is not built by asking about the self, but by: • narrating place, • explaining systems, • offering local knowledge.

Personal curiosity unlocks only after the other person volunteers information first.

⸻

  1. Trust Is Built Through Systems, Not Selves

Midwestern trust formation centers on competence and stewardship, not disclosure.

Accepted conversation anchors include: • infrastructure, • agriculture, • logistics, • geology, • co-ops, • and how things are prevented from failing.

Questions about aquifers, crop rotation, or grain silos signal seriousness and respect far more than personal questions.

⸻

  1. Aquifer Culture, Not River Culture

The Midwest interior is shaped by invisible water. • Surface rivers define borders and export routes, not daily life. • Water security comes from deep aquifers, not visible flow. • Risk is delayed, invisible, and system-managed.

This produces a worldview that prizes: • long-term planning, • stability, • boring competence, • and avoidance of sudden pressure changes.

River cultures create narrative, hierarchy, and relational flow. Aquifer cultures create systems.

⸻

  1. Institutions Anchor Life Quietly

Churches function as civic infrastructure rather than moral theaters. • High church density exists without hellfire or emotional drama. • Church keeps time, continuity, and care networks intact. • Emotional intensity is displaced into sports, which function as the safe outlet for passion and conflict.

⸻

  1. Belonging Is Provisional and Unceremonious

There are no initiation rituals, no open suspicion, and no moment where one is declared “in.”

Instead: • people are treated as equivalent by default, • trust is neither granted nor withheld explicitly, • belonging accrues through repetition, reliability, and time.

This is unsettling to someone used to negotiated boundaries but is not hostile—just non-narrated.

docs.title: Old Scripts
docs.text:
0 4 * * * rsync -ahq --no-links --delete /home/abby/Projects/ /media/abby/DARPA/projects/ >> /home/abby/logs/projects-backup.log 2>&1

0 2 * * * rsync -ahq --no-links --delete abby@smersh.local:/var/www/ /media/abby/DARPA/www/ >> /home/abby/logs/www-backup.log 2>&1

docs.title: Snapshot
docs.text:
Files in smersh:/etc/nginx are backed up to DARPA/archive/nginx

rsync -ahq --no-links \
   abby@smersh.local:/etc/nginx/ \ 
 /media/abby/DARPA/archive/nginx/

Before shutdown, this is what's listening now:

Listening output

docs.title: Stopping and disabling Nginx
docs.text:
Nginx prevents it from grabbing ports on boot

sudo systemctl stop nginx

sudo systemctl disable nginx
Synchronizing state of nginx.service with SysV service script with /usr/lib/systemd/systemd-sysv-install.
Executing: /usr/lib/systemd/systemd-sysv-install disable nginx
Removed '/etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/nginx.service'.

docs.title: Confirm ports are free
docs.text:
sudo ss -ltnp | egrep ':(80|443)\b' || echo "80/443 free"
80/443 free

docs.title: Install instructions from Caddy Documentation
docs.text:
sudo apt install -y debian-keyring debian-archive-keyring apt-transport-https curl
curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudsmith.io/public/caddy/stable/gpg.key' | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/caddy-stable-archive-keyring.gpg
sudo chmod o+r /usr/share/keyrings/caddy-stable-archive-keyring.gpg
sudo chmod o+r /etc/apt/sources.list.d/caddy-stable.list
sudo apt update
sudo apt install caddy

Caddy install commands

docs.title: Debian
docs.text:
Install the prerequisites. Most of these should already be installed. The command will only add the missing components not duplicate anything already existing.

sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y php-fpm php-cli php-xml php-mbstring php-gd php-curl php-zip php-mysql php-intl php-bcmath unzip git mariadb-server

Check PHP version and that FPM is running.

php -v
systemctl status php*-fpm --no-pager

docs.title: Install Composer
docs.text:
This will install Composer system-wide.

cd /tmp
php -r "copy('https://getcomposer.org/installer', 'composer-setup.php');"

php composer-setup.php

All settings correct for using Composer
Downloading...

Composer (version 2.9.3) successfully installed to: /tmp/composer.phar
Use it: php composer.phar

sudo mv composer.phar /usr/local/bin/composer
composer --version

Composer version 2.9.3 2025-12-30 13:40:17
PHP version 8.4.11 (/usr/bin/php8.4)
Run the "diagnose" command to get more detailed diagnostics output.

docs.title: Create Drupal Directory
docs.text:
Create the directory

mkdir /var/www/abbynet/drupal
cd /var/www/abbynet/drupal

Create the project

cd /var/www/abbynet/drupal
composer create-project drupal/recommended-project:^10 .

docs.title: Directory Permissions
docs.text:
Drupal will want this directory to exist before install

sudo mkdir -p /var/www/abbynet/drupal/web/sites/default/files
sudo chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/abbynet/drupal/web/sites/default/files

docs.title: Create Database
docs.text:
CREATE DATABASE drupal CHARACTER SET utf8mb4 COLLATE utf8mb4_general_ci;
CREATE USER 'drupaluser'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'use-a-long-password-here';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON drupal.* TO 'drupaluser'@'localhost';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
EXIT;

docs.title: Caddy Site Block
docs.text:
drupal.cloverdalelane.com {
    root * /var/www/abbynet/drupal/web

    basic_auth {
        abby <password hash>
    }

    php_fastcgi unix//run/php/php8.4-fpm.sock
    file_server
}

To find the php socket version:

ls -l /run/php/

docs.title: Reload Caddy
docs.text:
sudo caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
sudo systemctl reload caddy

docs.title: Install Drupal
docs.text:
Navigate to:

https://drupal.example.com

It should show the Drupal installer. Use the DB settings from earlier:

  • Database: drupal
  • Username: drupaluser
  • Password: the one set
  • Host: localhost

docs.title: Sanity Checks
docs.text:
See if PHP is being executed:

curl -I https://drupal.cloverdalelane.com

Check Caddy logs:

journalctl -u caddy -n 100 --no-pager

Check php-fpm logs:

journalctl -u php8.2-fpm -n 100 --no-pager

docs.title: Gender Designation Change Letter (Iowa)
docs.text:
To Whom It May Concern:

I, [physician’s name], am a licensed physician in the State of Alabama and the primary care physician of Abigail Jane Morton (formerly Kurt Sanford Morton). [Describe physician’s qualifications and medical background, if desired].

I have treated Abigail Jane Morton (formerly Kurt Sanford Morton) since [date] for the purpose of completing gender transition and a permanent sex designation change from male to female. These treatments and medical procedures included, but were not limited to, [provide detailed description of physical, mental, or psychological treatments and procedures, including drugs, hormones, surgery, etc.]. The treatments irreversibly altered [his or her] body in the following manner: [include a detailed description of results of surgery or non-surgical treatments, e.g., development of breast tissue, decreased size of testicles, inhibition of sperm maturation, or enlargement of clitoris, deepening of voice, growth of facial hair, hair loss at the temples and crown of the head, increased growth and thickening of body hair, etc.].

All of the treatments Abigail Jane Morton (formerly Kurt Sanford Morton) received under my care were medically necessary, clinically appropriate, and in accord with the standards for treatment of patient's diagnosis of gender dysphoria and the guidelines of the World Professional Association for Transgender Health, American Medical Association, American Psychiatric Association, American Psychological Association, and the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. Sex reassignment surgery was not medically necessary, clinically appropriate, or consistent with the above-mentioned standards and Guidelines to complete the sex designation change or gender transition of Abigail Jane Morton (formerly Kurt Sanford Morton).

In my professional medical opinion and judgment, the sex designation of Abigail Jane Morton (formerly Kurt Sanford Morton) has been permanently changed. Please contact me if you have questions or need more information. I declare under penalty of perjury under the laws of the United States and the State of [State] that the forgoing is true and correct.

Respectfully submitted, [Name of Physician] [State Medical License #] [DEA License #] [Address and Contact Information]

STATE OF Alabama COUNTY OF Tuscaloosa This record was acknowledged before me on [date] by [name of physician]. Notary Public in and for the State of Alabama My commission expires on _________

docs.title: Markdown Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
Thanks for visiting The Markdown Guide!

This Markdown cheat sheet provides a quick overview of all the Markdown syntax elements. It can’t cover every edge case, so if you need more information about any of these elements, refer to the reference guides for basic syntax and extended syntax.

docs.title: Basic Syntax
docs.text: These are the elements outlined in John Gruber’s original design document. All Markdown applications support these elements.

docs.title: Bold
docs.text: bold text

docs.title: Italic
docs.text: italicized text

docs.title: Blockquote
docs.text: blockquote

docs.title: Ordered List
docs.text:
1. First item
  2. Second item
  3. Third item

docs.title: Unordered List
docs.text:
• First item
  • Second item
  • Third item

docs.title: Code
docs.text: code

docs.title: Link
docs.text: Markdown Guide

docs.title: Extended Syntax
docs.text: These elements extend the basic syntax by adding additional features. Not all Markdown applications support these elements.

docs.title: Table
docs.text: Syntax Description Header Title Paragraph Text

{
  "firstName": "John",
  "lastName": "Smith",
  "age": 25
}

docs.title: Create Tables
docs.text:
These are the queries used to create the tables in the database:

    CREATE TABLE leagues (
          league_id INT UNSIGNED NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
          sport ENUM('soccer','american_football','baseball','basketball','cricket') NOT NULL,
          league_name VARCHAR(120) NOT NULL,
          country VARCHAR(80) NULL,
          level VARCHAR(50) NULL,
          is_active TINYINT(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
          PRIMARY KEY (league_id),
          UNIQUE KEY uq_league (sport, league_name)
        ) ENGINE=InnoDB DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4;

 CREATE TABLE teams (
  team_id INT UNSIGNED NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  sport ENUM('soccer','american_football','baseball','basketball','cricket') NOT NULL,
  team_name VARCHAR(120) NOT NULL,
  short_name VARCHAR(40) NULL,
  country VARCHAR(80) NULL,
  is_active TINYINT(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  PRIMARY KEY (team_id),
  KEY idx_team_sport (sport, team_name)
) ENGINE=InnoDB DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4;

CREATE TABLE user_follows (
  follow_id INT UNSIGNED NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  follow_type ENUM('team','league') NOT NULL,
  team_id INT UNSIGNED NULL,
  league_id INT UNSIGNED NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (follow_id),
  UNIQUE KEY uq_follow_team (follow_type, team_id),
  UNIQUE KEY uq_follow_league (follow_type, league_id),
  CONSTRAINT fk_follow_team FOREIGN KEY (team_id) REFERENCES teams(team_id),
  CONSTRAINT fk_follow_league FOREIGN KEY (league_id) REFERENCES leagues(league_id)
) ENGINE=InnoDB DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4;

docs.title: New York City, Police, Transit, and Thoughts
docs.text:
Dear Mayor Mamdani,

I am writing to propose a straightforward fiscal and governance reform regarding subway fare enforcement that would save New York City money, reduce unnecessary policing, and better align responsibility with authority.

At present, fare evasion is a revenue problem borne by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority, a New York State public authority. However, the primary cost of enforcement is borne by the New York City Police Department, a city agency funded by the City of New York. This structural misalignment produces a perverse and well-documented outcome: the City spends more policing fare evasion than the MTA loses from fare evasion itself.

This is not a matter of ideology or public safety “vibes.” It is a budgeting failure. The entity that controls fares and benefits from enforcement does not bear the cost of enforcing them. As a result, enforcement is overused even when it fails a basic cost-benefit test.

One possible avenue is state reimbursement for subway fare enforcement.

The most immediate and realistic fix is for New York State to reimburse New York City for NYPD resources devoted to fare enforcement in the subway system. This could include overtime, dedicated patrols, administrative processing, and related court costs.

Once enforcement costs are internalized by the MTA or the State, policy decisions would quickly become more rational. If enforcement truly pays for itself, reimbursement will confirm that. If it does not, the current strategy would be reevaluated on its actual merits rather than political optics. Either outcome improves fiscal discipline and transparency.

Barring that, honestly, the preferable option is to transfer the MTA to city control.

If the State is unwilling to reimburse the City for enforcing a state agency’s revenue policy, the logical alternative is to realign authority entirely by placing the MTA under city control. This would ensure that fare policy, enforcement decisions, and budgetary consequences sit within the same governance structure.

I recognize that this option is politically complex and unlikely in the near term. I raise it not as a demand, but as a reminder of the underlying principle: policy authority and financial responsibility must be aligned, or inefficiency is guaranteed.

New York City should not be subsidizing state-level political priorities through its police budget. Asking the State to pay for the enforcement it demands is not radical. It is sound public finance.

Thank you for your attention, and for your leadership on issues where fiscal reality and human dignity intersect.

Respectfully,

Abby Jane

docs.title: On Identity
docs.text:
Being a woman means having the capacity to endure asymmetry without complaint.

Before we wrap up, I want to share one personal but relevant detail. I go by Abby Jane. I am transgender, and while some of my materials still reflect my legal name, this is the name I use in the workplace and the one I would expect to use here.

“Before we conclude, there’s one piece of clarity I want to offer so there are no surprises later.

Professionally, I go by Abby Jane. I’m transgender, and while some materials still reflect my legal name, this is the identity I would be bringing to the role.

I value transparency, and I want to make sure we’re aligned now rather than discovering misalignment down the line.”

docs.title: Idea
docs.text:
On a specific day of the month (the second Wednesday; the 17th, etc.) people are invited to bring a soup/chili stew to share in the spirit of queer comraderie. People are encouraged to show up even if they haven't brought anything, but an offer to help clean or set up is appreciated.

You don't have to be queer to participate, but this would be a queer space, so attendees are expected to comport themselves accordingly.

docs.title: Plan
docs.text:
• Use the Moon Room at Druid City Brewery.
  • People will sign up to bring soups/stews/chilis
  • Others will sign up to bring utensils, napkins, crackers, etc.
  • Some may sign up to help set up or clean up

The evening should be chill. Just eating and talking. I don't think there would be need for a band or entertainment (maybe get the bar to play something appropriate or people can bring vinyl?).

docs.title: Promotion
docs.text:
• Flyers around the bar and other places where interested people might congregate (The Alcove, Icon, the University Campus).
  • Website for signups
  • Social media posts
  • Word of mouth

docs.title: Website
docs.text:
• Spec out a Google Forms invitation that has the correct logic
  • Build framework around it
  • Try to find a self-hosted system or one that is GitHub pages friendly

docs.title: Fundraising
docs.text:
• People can donate to help fund the soup get-together, and/or
  • A local support group for LGBTQ+ kids in need

docs.title: Schedule
docs.text:
5 Days | Tokyo → Kyoto → Hiroshima → Hakone → Tokyo Birthday = Day 7

Rhythm key: - 🌸 Soft = decompression, wandering, cafés - 🌿 Medium = structured but gentle - 🔥 Intense = emotionally or physically heavy anchor

docs.title: DAY 1 — Arrive Tokyo
docs.text:
🌸 Soft | Decompression Day

  • Arrive Narita or Haneda
  • Train into city, hotel check- in
  • Shower, change
  • 20–40 min neighborhood walk
  • Convenience- store dinner (onigiri, miso, tea)
  • Early night

Goal: land safely in your body

docs.title: DAY 2 — Asakusa + Ueno
docs.text:
🌿 Medium

  • Senso-ji Temple
  • Nakamise shopping street
  • Ueno Park
  • Tokyo National Museum (optional)
  • Coffee + cake stop
  • Dinner near hotel

docs.title: DAY 3 — Shibuya + Harajuku + Meiji Shrine
docs.text:
🔥 Intense (Urban Sensory Day)

  • Meiji Jingu forest walk
  • Harajuku wandering
  • Shibuya Crossing (once, reverently)
  • Loft or Tokyu Hands1
  • Daikanyama dinner

docs.title: DAY 4 — Museums + Bookstores
docs.text:
🌸 Soft

  • Mori Art Museum
  • Jimbocho used bookstores
  • Stationery stores
  • Café hopping
  • Long bath + skincare night

docs.title: DAY 5 — Neighborhood Day (Shimokitazawa or Koenji)
docs.text:
🌿 Medium

  • Vintage shops
  • Indie bookstores
  • Vinyl shops
  • People-watching cafés
  • Dinner at a tiny local place

docs.title: DAY 6 — Flex / Rest / Rain Buffer
docs.text:
🌸 Soft

  • Anything you skipped
  • Light shopping
  • Hair or spa
  • Or literally nothing

docs.title: DAY 7 — 🎂 Birthday Day in Tokyo
docs.text:
🔥 Intense (But Romantic, Not Heavy)

(This is your sacred pivot day.)

  • Morning shrine visit (gratitude ritual)
  • Solo gift purchase for yourself
  • Fancy lunch
  • Nezu Museum
  • Sunset river walk
  • High- end dessert café
  • Champagne in your hotel room

docs.title: DAY 8 — Tokyo → Kyoto
docs.text:
🌿 Medium (Transit + Arrival)

  • Bullet train to Kyoto
  • Hotel check- in
  • Kamo River walk
  • Dinner in Pontocho
  • Early night

docs.title: DAY 9 — Eastern Kyoto Temples
docs.text:
🔥 Intense (Spiritual + Physical)

  • Kiyomizu-dera
  • Sannenzaka / Ninenzaka
  • Yasaka Shrine
  • Tea ceremony
  • Long slow dinner

docs.title: DAY 10 — Philosopher’s Path + Ginkaku- ji
docs.text:
🌸 Soft

  • Canal walk
  • Silver Pavilion
  • Bookshop café
  • Journaling afternoon
  • Light dinner

docs.title: DAY 11 — Arashiyama
docs.text:
🌿 Medium

  • Bamboo grove (early)
  • Tenryu- ji
  • River walk
  • Monkey park (optional)
  • Afternoon nap

docs.title: DAY 12 — Kyoto → Hiroshima
docs.text:
🌿 Medium (Transit + Emotional Setup)

  • Bullet train to Hiroshima
  • Hotel check- in
  • Evening river walk
  • Quiet dinner
  • Early night

docs.title: DAY 13 — Hiroshima Peace Day
docs.text:
🔥 Intense (Moral Anchor Day)

  • Peace Memorial Park
  • Atomic Bomb Dome
  • Hiroshima Peace Museum
  • Long silent river walk
  • Simple dinner
  • Early bed

(You will be emotionally done. This is correct.)

docs.title: DAY 14 — Hiroshima → Hakone
docs.text:
🌿 Medium (Transit + Reset)

  • Morning train to Hakone
  • Ryokan check- in
  • Private onsen
  • Kaiseki dinner
  • Early night

docs.title: DAY 15 — Hakone → Tokyo → Fly Home
docs.text:
🌸 Soft (Closure Day)

  • Early onsen soak
  • Train to Tokyo
  • Airport train
  • Reflection
  • Cry softly on the plane

  1. Two department stores ↩

I’d love for our team to get plugged into the Ellucian forums — other registrars post templates, workflows, and automation ideas there every week. We don’t have to reinvent the wheel.”

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This framework is intended as a practical tool for registrar leadership teams undertaking SIS modernization, ERP upgrades, or moving to cloud and/or SaaS environments. It offers a structured way to inventory, evaluate, and intentionally retain or retire customizations without flattening legitimate academic complexity.

The goal is not to reduce customization for its own sake, but to make institutional complexity explicit, governable, and sustainable over time.

docs.title: Guiding Principle
docs.text:
What is "de-customization":

Systematically reducing institution-specific logic that increases technical debt, impedes upgrades, and embeds obsolete policy assumptions, while preserving the academic values that motivated the customizations in the first place.

docs.title: Framework
docs.text:
Questions to guide the process:

  1. What customizations do we have?

  2. What are the parameters of the customization?

  3. What limitations of the baseline SIS made the customization necessary?

  4. Is the process for which the customization was built still used; has it materially changed since implementation?

  5. For each customization, is it still necessary, is the process simply unable to be incorporated into baseline, if not, what changes can be reasonably made to the process so it can be integrated into baseline?

docs.title: 1. Inventory and Visibility
docs.text:
Identify all non-baseline elements, including:

  • Database-level modifications
  • Custom scripts / middleware
  • Configuration overrides that behave like code
  • Manual workarounds that function as “shadow customizations”

Document:

  • Owner
  • Date implemented
  • Triggering requirement (policy, accreditation, faculty demand, historical artifact)

docs.title: 2. Define the Scope and Nature of Each Customization
docs.text:
Classify each customization as one of:

  • Policy-driven (e.g., grading exceptions, registration priority)
  • Pedagogical (tutorial systems, narrative evaluation workflows)
  • Compliance-driven (VA, financial aid, visa tracking)
  • Historical workaround (baseline once lacked a feature)
  • Cultural preference (“we’ve always done it this way”)

This matters because only some categories deserve preservation pressure.

docs.title: 3. Interrogate the Original Constraint
docs.text:
Was baseline truly incapable, or merely unfamiliar?

Has the vendor since:

  • Added baseline support?
  • Shifted philosophy (e.g., from transactional to workflow-driven)?

Was the customization compensating for:

  • Weak governance
  • Policy ambiguity
  • Faculty needs that have since changed

Has the system changed, but the customization hasn't?

docs.title: 4. Assess Process Drift
docs.text:
For each customization, is the underlying academic or administrative process:

  • Still in use?
  • Materially changed?
  • Under active debate?

Has the customization:

  • Frozen a process that should have evolved?
  • Become de facto policy?

docs.title: 5. Decision Matrix: Retire, Refactor, Retain
docs.text:
For each customization:

  1. Retire
  2. Criteria
    • If baseline now supports it
    • If the process no longer exists
    • Requires
      • Policy owner sign-off
      • Data migration or archival plan
      • Communication to affected stakeholders
  3. Refactor into configuration
    • Criteria
      • If logic can be expressed via delivered tools
    • Requires
      • Vendor validation
      • Test environment modeling
      • Rollback plan
      • Documentation update
      • Training for users
  4. Retain (explicity and defensively)
    • Criteria
    • If it reflects a core academic value
    • If eliminating it would materially harm pedagogy
    • Requires
      • Named business owner
      • Justification tied to institutional mission
      • Upgrade/migration impact assessment
      • Timeline for periodic review

The guiding principle is that retention becomes an intentional act, not simply inertia.

docs.title: Keys
docs.text:
1. Faculty, student, and staff needs are real and important.
  2. Academic exceptionalism is a feature.
  3. The staff/faculty own the logic.
  4. No one is being replaced.
  5. Technical debt is necessary only when it serves a purpose that coincides with the academic, cultural, and social mission of the College.
  6. Systems encode values whether we intend them to or not.

docs.title: Quotes
docs.text:
Many institutions were afraid to touch student systems, especially customizations, because no one remembered why they existed. That’s where the real risk lives.

The goal isn’t to remove what works, but to understand it well enough that we can support it sustainably, upgrade safely, and explain it clearly to the next group of people who inherit it.

The goal not here to simplify the College. Rather, it is to make its complexity intentional and sustainable.

The first thing to understand is: what in the system matters most to the College, and what would feel like a loss if we got it wrong?

Abby Jane Morton Prepared as part of a University Registrar search process January 2026

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: An organization that operates in a high-risk, complex, tightly coupled environment and nevertheless achieves an exceptionally low rate of catastrophic failure over long periods of time, not because it eliminates error, but because it continuously detects, contains, and learns from it.

docs.title: Why This Matters for Registrars
docs.text:
Registrar operations share the same risk profile as classic high-reliability environments: low tolerance for error, tightly coupled processes, irreversible downstream consequences, and deadline-driven surges.

Treating the registrar’s office explicitly as a high-reliability organization reframes everyday academic administration as safety-critical work rather than clerical throughput.

docs.title: What it is.
docs.text:
• Preoccupation with failure
  • Errors, near-misses, anomalies are signals
  • Success today doesn't imply safety tomorrow
  • Actively looks for what almost goes wrong This is the opposite of "no news is good news."
  • Reluctance to simplify interpretations
  • Avoids single-cause explanations
  • Encourages multiple perspectives
  • Resists "this is an unimportant process" This is directly counter to the "operator error" reflex.
  • Sensitivity to operations
  • Maintains situational awareness of front-line processes
  • Values operational/experiential insight over abstract metrics
  • Knows what the work looks like not just what the procedures dictate This is where most bureaucracies fail.
  • Commitment to resilience
  • Assumes things will go wrong
  • Trains for both recovery and prevention
  • Practices graceful degradation rather than brittle perfection This is key to "normal accident" prevention.
  • Deference to expertise
  • Authority rests with the person who has the most relevant knowledge not position
  • Rank yields to situational competence
  • Escalation is normalized, encouraged, and, most importantly, not punished The appropriate person makes and documents the decision.

docs.title: What it is not
docs.text:
• Cultures that expect zero defects
  • Obtuse or jargon heavy documentation
  • Rigidly hierarchical
  • Compliance-heavy bureaucracies
  • Check-list obsessed
  • Punishment-driven

docs.title: The Registrar's office's role
docs.text:
A registrar’s office is responsible for records whose correctness must be near-perfect1, at scale2, under constant change3, with non-negotiable deadlines4 and asymmetric consequences5.

1. An institution is only as credible as its records. 2. Records arrive in volume. 3. Institutions change unexpectedly. 4. Enforcement of deadlines is a core mission of the office. 5. Small errors can have huge downstream harm.

docs.title: High Reliability Registrar's Office
docs.text:
• Preoccupation with failure
  • Assume the system will break somewhere.
  • In practice:
    • Near misses are signals even if the end-result was successful
    • Actively tracks errors and error origination
    • Design processes that expect mistakes or malfunctions of systems
    • Dual controls for high-impact processes (degree posting, academic standing)
  • Procedural implementation:
    • Validation rules that block action not just warn
    • Routine auditing of data and processes
  • Staff implications
    • Staff are actively encouraged to surface problems without fearing blame
    • "I think I made a mistake" is expected and rewarded rather than punished
  • Reluctance to simplify interpretations
  • Resist the urge to flatten complexity.
  • In practice:
    • Edge cases are not considered "close enough"
    • Document a process's exceptions and quirks
    • Know that policy can be technically simple but operationally complex
  • Procedural implementation:
    • Clear exception pathways (no shadow processes or tribal knowledge)
    • Documentation that explains both why and how
    • Systems designed to reflect nuance instead of implementing workarounds
  • Staff implications
    • Staff are empowered actors not button-pushers
    • Expertise and correctness is valued over speed
    • Senior staff are consulted not because of rank, but because of accumulated pattern recognition
  • Sensitivity to operations
  • Stay close to the work as it actually happens.
  • In practice:
    • Leadership understands and plans for peak stress points
    • Decisions are based on peak stress points not quiet times
  • Procedural implementation
    • Visibility of queues and bottlenecks
    • Clearly delineated roles within the office and between other offices
    • Term calendars treated as an operational risk map, not just procedural dates
  • Staff implications
    • Managers observe workflows (both physical and electronic)
    • Staff are part of the process change system
    • "This will break" is taken seriously
  • Commitment to resilience
  • We don’t just prevent failure; we recover gracefully.
  • In practice:
    • Recognize the office already does crisis response
    • High reliability means formalization of that response
  • Procedural implementation
    • Documentation includes failure and recovery playbooks; what to do in case of catastrophe
    • Cross-training is routine and formalized
    • Versioning of documentation; what changed when did it change
  • Staff implications
    • Staff are empowered and trained to diagnose and respond
    • Institutional memory is written not kept as individual knowledge
    • Practiced recovery is planned for and the need for heroics are minimized
  • Deference to expertise
  • Authority rests with whomever knows the most right now.
  • In practice:
    • The person who understands a process leads during peak times
    • All staff are involved in integration and testing
    • The person who understands a process handles edge cases
    • For the preceding, rank and title are irrelevant and ignored
  • Procedural implementation
    • Clear escalation paths based on expertise not rank
    • Decisions are documented and include why a decision was made
    • Elimination on single points of failure
  • Staff implications
    • Roles are defined by responsibility and expertise
    • Career paths emphasize systems thinking and policy mastery
    • Anyone and everyone is empowered to yell STOP

docs.title: What does this mean?
docs.text:
• Staff are:
  • data stewards
  • policy translators
  • quality assurance professionals

  • risk managers

  • Automation:

  • doesn't devalue people; it elevates them
  • staff focus is on interpretation, exception handling, and system oversight
  • Success is measure by error prevention and recovery; not processing volume

The Conclusion

A registrar’s office is already a high-reliability organization; the work only becomes sustainable when we design processes, roles, organizational culture, and governance structures that treat accuracy, resilience, and expertise as the core mission, not side effects.

Abby Jane Morton Prepared as part of a University Registrar search process January 2026

docs.title: Overriding Beliefs
docs.text:
• Student-first means removing friction from the people who serve students directly.
  • Technology is a means not an end unto itself.
  • A goal of mine is that no one using the systems I oversee knows I exist.
  • My responsibility isn't only to the student in front of me; but all future, current, and former students; every student deserves credentials that are credible
